options.A: an array of derivative positions managed separately from securities portfolio to achieve overall portfolio characteristics desired by manager — bottom-up strategies can lead to unintended macro (sector or country) risk exposures.
options.B: Portfolio overlays provide no risk-control function for bottom-up fundamental investors, whose security-level analysis already addresses every dimension of portfolio-level exposure.
options.C: Overlays serve no identifiable purpose in equity portfolio management and are treated in the reading as a legacy practice that modern construction techniques have made obsolete.

rationales.A:
Full statement: an array of derivative positions managed separately from securities portfolio to achieve overall portfolio characteristics desired by manager — bottom-up strategies can lead to unintended macro (sector or country) risk exposures; investor may adjust sector weights to align with benchmark's weights via long and short positions in derivatives; overlays can also add active returns not correlated with underlying portfolio strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Bottom-up fundamental strategies often lead to unintended macro (e.g., sector or country) risk exposures. However, bottom-up fundamental investors can incorporate some of the risk control benefits of top-down investment strategies via portfolio overlays. (A portfolio overlay is an array of derivative positions managed separately from the securities portfolio to achieve overall portfolio characteristics that are desired by the portfolio manager.) The fundamental investor's sector weights, for example, may vary from the benchmark's weights as a result of the stock selection process even though the investor did not intend to make sector bets. In that case, the investor may be able to adjust the sector weights to align with the benchmark's weights via long and short positions in derivatives. In this way, top-down strategies can be effective in controlling risk exposures. Overlays can also be used to attempt to add active returns that are not correlated with those generated by the underlying portfolio strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Risk control is the point — derivatives adjust aggregate exposures without disturbing the stock selections.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The overlay shapes total-portfolio characteristics (beta, country, currency) around the securities book.

options.A: identify significant factors that can predict future stock returns and construct a portfolio that tilts towards such factors.
options.B: Factor-based strategies aim to eliminate all factor exposure from the portfolio.
options.C: Factor-based strategies pursue no defined objective.

rationales.A:
Full statement: identify significant factors that can predict future stock returns and construct a portfolio that tilts towards such factors; some rely on single factor (transparent, stable exposure with regular rebalancing); others rely on selection of factors; some attempt to time factor exposure recognizing that factor performance varies over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Broadly defined, a factor-based strategy aims to identify significant factors that can predict future stock returns and to construct a portfolio that tilts towards such factors. Some strategies rely on a single factor, are transparent, and maintain a relatively stable exposure to that factor with regular rebalancing (as is explained in the curriculum reading on passive equity investing). Other strategies rely on a selection of factors. Yet other strategies may attempt to time the exposure to factors, recognizing that factor performance varies over time.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Eliminating factor exposure describes market-NEUTRAL construction; factor strategies deliberately TILT toward predictive factors.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The objective is explicit: identify return-predictive factors and tilt the portfolio toward them.

options.A: The hedged portfolio approach uses a single quantile, holding only the top-ranked stocks long.
options.B: after choosing factor and ranking investable stock universe by that factor, divide universe into quantiles (typically quintiles or deciles) to form quantile portfolios.
options.C: The Fama-French approach involves no ranking of the investable universe.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The construction is long the TOP quantile and SHORT the BOTTOM quantile — two quantiles, hedged.
rationales.B:
Full statement: after choosing factor and ranking investable stock universe by that factor, divide universe into quantiles (typically quintiles or deciles) to form quantile portfolios; long/short hedged portfolio formed by going long best quantile and shorting worst quantile; performance tracked over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The most traditional and widely used method for implementing factor-based portfolios is the hedged portfolio approach, pioneered and formulated by Fama and French (1993). In this approach, after choosing the factor to be scrutinized and ranking the investable stock universe by that factor, investors divide the universe into groups referred to as quantiles (typically quintiles or deciles) to form quantile portfolios. Stocks are either equally weighted or capitalization weighted within each quantile. A long/short hedged portfolio is typically formed by going long the best quantile and shorting the worst quantile. The performance of the hedged long/short portfolio is then tracked over time.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Factor-based ranking into quintiles or deciles is the first step of the method.

options.A: quality factor based on earnings quality — Sloan proposed that stock prices fail to reflect fully the information contained in accrual and cash flow components of current earnings.
options.B: No accruals-based factor exists in the quantitative literature.
options.C: Sloan's work concerned momentum, not earnings quality.

rationales.A:
Full statement: quality factor based on earnings quality — Sloan proposed that stock prices fail to reflect fully the information contained in accrual and cash flow components of current earnings; performance of accruals anomaly factor appears to be quite cyclical; long-term cash flows from operations and net income should converge and be consistent (but can diverge short-term as management has more discretion in accruals-based accounting).

Correct. Per the reading: 'One of the best-known examples of how in-depth accounting knowledge can impact investment performance is Richard Sloan's (1996) seminal paper on earnings quality, with its proposition of the accruals factor. Sloan suggests that stock prices fail to reflect fully the information contained in the accrual and cash flow components of current earnings. The performance of the accruals anomaly factor, however, appears to be quite cyclical.' Footnote: 'Sloan (1996) argues that in the long term, cash flows from operations and net income (under accruals-based accounting) should converge and be consistent. In the short term, they could diverge. Management has more discretion in accruals-based accounting; therefore, the temporary divergence between cash flows and net income reflects how conservative a company chooses to be in reporting its net income.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sloan (1996) established the accruals factor — a quality signal from the accrual/cash-flow mix of earnings.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The finding is that prices under-react to the ACCRUAL composition of earnings — an earnings-quality effect.

options.A: Style rotation assumes factor performance is constant across all periods, making rotation unnecessary.
options.B: based on belief that different factors (size, value, momentum, quality) work well during some time periods but less well during others.
options.C: Equity style rotation strategies serve no investment purpose.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The premise is the opposite — factors cycle, working in some periods and lagging in others.
rationales.B:
Full statement: based on belief that different factors (size, value, momentum, quality) work well during some time periods but less well during others; use process that allocates to stock baskets representing each style when style expected to offer positive excess return vs benchmark; generally more in domain of quantitative investing; classification of securities into style categories is less standardized than sector or country allocation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Equity style rotation strategies, a subcategory of factor investing, are based on the belief that different factors — such as size, value, momentum, and quality — work well during some time periods but less well during other time periods. These strategies use an investment process that allocates to stock baskets representing each of these styles when a particular style is expected to offer a positive excess return compared to the benchmark. While style rotation as a strategy can be used in both fundamental and quantitative investment processes, it is generally more in the domain of quantitative investing. Unlike sector or country allocation, discussed earlier, the classification of securities into style categories is less standardized.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The purpose is timing exposures toward whichever factors the regime currently favors.

options.A: Activist investors avoid all engagement with company management to preserve independence.
options.B: The reading identifies no strategy resembling shareholder activism.
options.C: specialize in taking stakes in listed companies and advocating changes for the purpose of producing a gain on the investment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Engagement is the strategy — advocating changes at portfolio companies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Activism is treated as a distinct strategy: stakes taken to advocate change for investment gain.
rationales.C:
Full statement: specialize in taking stakes in listed companies and advocating changes for the purpose of producing a gain on the investment; may seek board representation or use other measures to initiate strategic, operational, or financial structure changes (asset sales, cost-cutting, management changes, capital structure changes, dividend increases, share buybacks); include hedge funds, public pension funds, private investors and vary greatly in approaches/expertise/horizons.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Activist investors specialize in taking stakes in listed companies and advocating changes for the purpose of producing a gain on the investment. The investor may wish to obtain representation on the company's board of directors or use other measures in an effort to initiate strategic, operational, or financial structure changes. In some cases, activist investors may support activities such as asset sales, cost-cutting measures, changes to management, changes to the capital structure, dividend increases, or share buybacks. Activists — including hedge funds, public pension funds, private investors, and others — vary greatly in their approaches, expertise, and investment horizons. They may also seek different outcomes. What they have in common is that they advocate for change in their target companies.'

options.A: typically less than 10% — activist investors aim to achieve their goals with smaller stakes rather than pursuing a full takeover bid.
options.B: Activist investors typically acquire majority control before advocating change.
options.C: Activism always culminates in a full takeover bid for the target.

rationales.A:
Full statement: typically less than 10% — activist investors aim to achieve their goals with smaller stakes rather than pursuing a full takeover bid; equity stakes generally made public; stakes above certain threshold must be made public in most jurisdictions; time horizon often shorter than buy-and-hold investors but whole process can last for a number of years.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Shareholder activism typically follows a period of screening and analysis of opportunities in the market. The investor usually reviews a number of companies based on a range of parameters and carries out in-depth analysis of the business and the opportunities for unlocking value. Activism itself starts when an investor buys an equity stake in the company and starts advocating for change (i.e., pursuing an activist campaign). These equity stakes are generally made public. Stakes above a certain threshold must be made public in most jurisdictions... The goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues). Rather than pursuing a full takeover bid, activist investors aim to achieve their goals with smaller stakes, typically of less than 10%. Activist investors' time horizon is often shorter than that of buy-and-hold investors, but the whole process can last for a number of years.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Typical stakes are UNDER 10% — influence, not control, is the tool.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Avoiding the cost of a full bid is part of the model; change is pursued from a minority position.

options.A: The HFRX Activist Index dramatically underperformed the S&P 500 on a risk-adjusted basis.
options.B: Activist index and S&P 500 performance were identical across return, volatility, and Sharpe ratio.
options.C: average annual return 7.7% with annual volatility 13.7% and Sharpe ratio 0.56 — slightly higher than S&P 500 Sharpe ratio of 0.54.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The comparison is close, with the activist index's Sharpe (0.56) slightly ABOVE the S&P 500's (0.54).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Return (7.7%) and volatility (13.7%) differ from the index; the Sharpe edge is slight but real.
rationales.C:
Full statement: average annual return 7.7% with annual volatility 13.7% and Sharpe ratio 0.56 — slightly higher than S&P 500 Sharpe ratio of 0.54; but difficult to conclude how much value activist investors add because HFRX index does not include large enough number of managers and managers vary in approaches and risks.

Correct. Per the reading's DO ACTIVIST INVESTORS GENERATE ALPHA? boxed section: 'Activist hedge funds are among the major activist investors. Based on the HFRX Activist Index, in the aggregate, activist hedge funds have delivered an average annual return of 7.7% with annual volatility of 13.7% and therefore a Sharpe ratio of 0.56 — slightly higher than the Sharpe ratio of the S&P 500 Index of 0.54 (see Exhibit 22). However, it is difficult to conclude how much value activist investors add because the HFRX index does not include a large enough number of managers. Furthermore, managers themselves vary in their approaches and the risks they take.'

options.A: target company stocks go up by 2% on average on announcement day (based on all activist events in Thomson Reuters Corporate Governance Intelligence database during discrete 30-year period).
options.B: Target stocks fall sharply on the announcement of activist involvement.
options.C: Markets show no measurable reaction to activist campaign announcements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: target company stocks go up by 2% on average on announcement day (based on all activist events in Thomson Reuters Corporate Governance Intelligence database during discrete 30-year period); positive reaction comes on top of stock appreciation prior to announcements; per Maug (1998), activists trade in stock prior to announcement to build up stake, assert control, and profit from value creation; modest post-announcement drift: month after activist announcement date, target share prices move up by 0.6% on average.

Correct. Per the reading's HOW DOES THE MARKET REACT TO ACTIVIST EVENTS? boxed section: 'Investors have generally reacted positively to activism announcements: On average, target company stocks go up by 2% on the announcement day (based on all activist events in the Thomson Reuters Corporate Governance Intelligence database during a discrete 30-year period). Interestingly, the positive reaction comes on top of stock appreciation prior to activism announcements... According to the model of Maug (1998), activist investors trade in a stock prior to the announcement to build up a stake, assert control, and profit from the value creation. It may also be argued that there must be information leakage about the activists' involvement, driving the stock higher even before the first public announcement. There is a modest post-announcement drift: In the month after the activist announcement date, target share prices move up by 0.6%, on average, relative to the market.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The average announcement-day move is POSITIVE — about +2%.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Thomson Reuters event data show a measurable +2% average pop on announcement day.

options.A: Activists target companies that are already outperforming their industries operationally.
options.B: companies with underperforming operations (below-industry return on assets), underutilized assets, potential for improvements via reducing fixed-asset investments, concentrating production, disposing of assets in line with peer practices.
options.C: No systematic pattern exists in the companies activists choose to target.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Targets typically show BELOW-industry returns on assets and underutilized assets — underperformance is the entry thesis.
rationales.B:
Full statement: companies with underperforming operations (below-industry return on assets), underutilized assets, potential for improvements via reducing fixed-asset investments, concentrating production, disposing of assets in line with peer practices; example (Trian Fund Management proposing splitting PepsiCo; Third Point calling for leadership change at Yahoo!); financial leverage of target companies increases significantly after activist involvement.

Correct. Per the reading Example 6 solution: 'Company C is the most appropriate choice. The company offers upside potential because of its ability to improve operating performance and cash payout using asset disposals, a strategy being implemented by other companies in its sector. Neither Company A nor Company B offers an attractive opportunity for activist investing: Company A is already operating efficiently, while Company B is more suitable for investors that focus on restructuring and distressed investing.' And footnote: 'Trian Fund Management proposed splitting PepsiCo into standalone public companies; Third Point called for leadership change at Yahoo!'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The pattern is clear: operational underperformers with improvement levers (costs, capital structure, payout).

options.A: Activist campaigns pursue financial gain exclusively; non-financial causes are never the objective.
options.B: The reading draws no distinction among activist objectives.
options.C: goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Environmental, social, and governance causes drive some campaigns — objectives can be non-financial.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The financial/non-financial goal distinction is drawn explicitly.
rationales.C:
Full statement: goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues); activist campaigns are typically diverse.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The goal of activist investing could be either financial gain (increased shareholder value) or a non-financial cause (e.g., environmental, social, and governance issues).' Activism can be motivated purely by return generation but also by ESG-oriented objectives that may or may not directly maximize shareholder returns.

options.A: identify target companies → buy target stock → study corporate structure/governance/shareholder base → submit proposal to company management → if agreement, exit or buy more.
options.B: The activist process consists of a single step: publicly demanding board seats.
options.C: The reading describes no structured activist process.

rationales.A:
Full statement: identify target companies → buy target stock → study corporate structure/governance/shareholder base → submit proposal to company management → if agreement, exit or buy more; if not, threaten proxy contest → negotiate settlement; if not agreement, launch proxy contest → shareholder vote.

Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 19 (A Typical Shareholder Activist Investing Process): shows the flow: 'Activist Investors Identify Target Companies → Buy Target Stock → Study Corporate Structure, Governance, Shareholder Base, etc. → Submit Proposal to Company Management → Reach Agreement? (yes: Exit or Buy More Stocks?; no: Threaten Proxy Contest) → Reach Agreement? (yes: Negotiate Settlement; no: Launch Proxy Contest) → Shareholder Vote.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The Exhibit 19 sequence runs from target identification and stake building through study, proposals, and escalation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. A defined multi-stage process is laid out, beginning with identifying targets and buying the stock.

options.A: Pairs trading is not a recognized strategy in statistical arbitrage.
options.B: Pairs trading involves holding a single security and trading around its own moving average.
options.C: identifies two securities historically highly correlated with each other — when price relationship deviates from long-term average, managers expecting temporary deviation go long the underperforming stock and simultaneously short the outperforming stock.

rationales.A: Incorrect. It is the canonical stat-arb strategy.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The unit is a correlated PAIR: short the outperformer, long the underperformer when the spread stretches, betting on reversion.
rationales.C:
Full statement: identifies two securities historically highly correlated with each other — when price relationship deviates from long-term average, managers expecting temporary deviation go long the underperforming stock and simultaneously short the outperforming stock; if prices converge to long-term average, investors close the trade and realize profit; bets on mean-reversion pattern; biggest risk is that observed divergence is structural (not temporary); risk management critical, so investors often employ stop-loss rules.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Pairs trading is an example of a popular and simple statistical arbitrage strategy. Pairs trading uses statistical techniques to identify two securities that are historically highly correlated with each other. When the price relationship of these two securities deviates from its long-term average, managers that expect the deviation to be temporary go long the underperforming stock and simultaneously short the outperforming stock. If the prices do converge to the long-term average as forecast, the investors close the trade and realize a profit. This kind of pairs trading therefore bets on a mean-reversion pattern in stock prices. The biggest risk in pairs trading and most other mean-reversion strategies is that the observed price divergence is not temporary; rather, it might be due to structural reasons. Because risk management is critical for the success of such strategies, investors often employ stop-loss rules to exit trades when a loss limit is reached.'

options.A: log price differential (spread = ln(CNR/CP)) with moving average computed on rolling 130-day window and bands at 2 standard deviations above and below moving average.
options.B: The CNR/CP example sets no boundaries on the spread for trade entry.
options.C: The example triggers trades on raw price differences with no statistical bands.

rationales.A:
Full statement: log price differential (spread = ln(CNR/CP)) with moving average computed on rolling 130-day window and bands at 2 standard deviations above and below moving average; simple strategy: enter trade when spread more than 2 SDs from moving average, close when spread reaches moving average again; correlation coefficient between the two stocks was 69% based on daily returns from 2 January 2014 to 26 May 2016.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Consider Canadian National Railway (CNR) and Canadian Pacific Railway (CP). These are the two dominant railways in Canada. Their business models are fairly similar, as both operate railway networks and transport goods throughout the country. Exhibit 24 shows that the prices of the two stocks have been highly correlated. The y-axis shows the log price differential, referred to as the spread [footnote: ln(Price of CNR/Price of CP)]. The exhibit also shows the moving average of the spread computed on a rolling 130-day window and bands at two standard deviations above and two standard deviations below the moving average. A simple pairs-trading strategy would be to enter into a trade when the spread is more than (or less than) two standard deviations from the moving average. The trade would be closed when the spread reaches the moving average again.' Correlation: 69% daily returns 2014-2016.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Entry uses defined bounds: ±2 standard deviations around the rolling mean of the log price spread.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The spread is the LOG ratio ln(CNR/CP), banded at 2 SDs on a rolling 130-day window.

options.A: All three CNR/CP trades were profitable, validating the strategy unconditionally.
options.B: first trade opened 2 October 2014 when spread crossed -2 SD mark, closed 18 November 2014 (profitable, position held slightly more than a month).
options.C: Exhibit 24 records no actual trades from the strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Results were MIXED — reversion is a bet, not a certainty, and structural breaks can defeat it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: first trade opened 2 October 2014 when spread crossed -2 SD mark, closed 18 November 2014 (profitable, position held slightly more than a month); second trade also profitable but lasted much longer; third trade entered 21 July 2015 experienced structural break — CP's decline further intensified while CNR stayed relatively flat, spread continued to narrow; loss on third trade could have been significantly greater than profits from first two if positions closed prior to mean reversion in spring 2016; example highlights risk inherent in mean-reversion strategies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Exhibit 24 shows the three trades based on our decision rules. The first trade was opened on 2 October 2014, when the spread between CNR and CP crossed the –2 standard deviation mark [footnote: The position is long CNR and short CP]. This trade was closed on 18 November 2014, when the spread reached the moving average. The first trade was profitable, and the position was maintained for slightly more than a month. The second trade was also profitable but lasted much longer. After the third trade was entered on 21 July 2015, however, there was a structural break, in that CP's decline further intensified while CNR stayed relatively flat; therefore, the spread continued to narrow. The loss on the third trade could have been significantly greater than the profits made from the first two transactions if the positions had been closed prior to mean reversion in the spring of 2016. This example highlights the risk inherent in mean-reversion strategies.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Three dated trades are shown, the first opened 2 October 2014 at the −2 SD band and closed profitably in November.

options.A: No US data source supports microstructure-based arbitrage research.
options.B: Microstructure arbitrage strategies operate on multi-month holding periods.
options.C: take advantage of NYSE Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and often involve extensive analysis of the limit order book to identify very short-term mispricing opportunities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The NYSE TAQ database is the standard source.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The opportunities live in the limit order book at very short — often millisecond — horizons.
rationales.C:
Full statement: take advantage of NYSE Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and often involve extensive analysis of the limit order book to identify very short-term mispricing opportunities; e.g., temporary imbalance between buy and sell orders may trigger price spike lasting only a few milliseconds; only investors with analytical tools and trading capabilities for high-frequency trading are in position to capture such opportunities, usually within portfolio of many stocks designed to take advantage of very short-term discrepancies.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the United States, many market microstructure–based arbitrage strategies take advantage of the NYSE Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and often involve extensive analysis of the limit order book to identify very short-term mispricing opportunities. For example, a temporary imbalance between buy and sell orders may trigger a spike in share price that lasts for only a few milliseconds. Only those investors with the analytical tools and trading capabilities for high-frequency trading are in a position to capture such opportunities, usually within a portfolio of many stocks designed to take advantage of very short-term discrepancies.'

options.A: The Example 8 mandate is value oriented, screening for low multiples and depressed prices.
options.B: growth orientation with focus on companies delivering EPS growth in recent years and maintaining earnings and price growth momentum — criterion 1 specifies accelerating EPS growth rates.
options.C: The example specifies no stylistic orientation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The screens target EPS growth and earnings/price momentum — a GROWTH orientation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: growth orientation with focus on companies delivering EPS growth in recent years and maintaining earnings and price growth momentum — criterion 1 specifies accelerating EPS growth rates; criterion 2 discards companies with negative recent earnings growth; criterion 3 screens for positive earnings surprise (past growth continuing); criteria 4 and 5 screen for positive recent stock price momentum; criterion 6 retains only stocks with at least average market liquidity; absence of valuation multiples among screening criteria (value investor's screening would typically include screen for expensive valuations).

Correct. Per Example 8 solution: 'The portfolio manager has a growth orientation with a focus on companies that have delivered EPS growth in recent years and that have maintained their earnings and price growth momentum. Criterion 1 specifies accelerating EPS growth rates over recent fiscal years, while criterion 2 discards companies for which recent earnings growth has been negative. Criterion 3 further screens for companies that have beaten consensus earnings expectations―have had a positive earnings surprise―in the most recent quarter. A positive earnings surprise suggests that past earnings growth is continuing. Criteria 4 and 5 screen for positive recent stock price momentum. Criterion 6 retains only stocks with at least average market liquidity. Note the absence of any valuation multiples among the screening criteria: A value investor's screening criteria would typically include a rule to screen out issues that are expensively valued relative to earnings or assets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The mandate is explicitly growth: recent EPS delivery plus maintained earnings and price momentum.

options.A: Confirmation bias is classified as an emotional bias rooted in fear of loss.
options.B: Confirmation bias does not exist as a recognized behavioral phenomenon.
options.C: cognitive error — tendency of analysts and investors to look for information that confirms existing beliefs about favorite companies and to ignore or undervalue any information that contradicts existing beliefs.

rationales.A: Incorrect. It is a COGNITIVE error — an information-processing failure, not an emotion-driven one.
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is among the best-documented biases: seeking confirming evidence and discounting contrary evidence on favored names.
rationales.C:
Full statement: cognitive error — tendency of analysts and investors to look for information that confirms existing beliefs about favorite companies and to ignore or undervalue any information that contradicts existing beliefs; creates selective exposure/perception/retention; consequences include poorly diversified portfolio, excessive risk exposure, holdings in poorly performing securities; can be reduced by actively seeking opinions of other investors/team members and looking for information from range of sources.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A cognitive error, confirmation bias — sometimes referred to as "stock love bias" — is the tendency of analysts and investors to look for information that confirms their existing beliefs about their favorite companies and to ignore or undervalue any information that contradicts their existing beliefs. This behavior creates selective exposure, perception, and retention and may be thought of as a selection bias. Some of the consequences are a poorly diversified portfolio, excessive risk exposure, and holdings in poorly performing securities. Actively seeking out the opinions of other investors or team members and looking for information from a range of sources to challenge existing beliefs may reduce the risk of confirmation bias.'

options.A: a cognitive error — 'an expectancy of a personal success probability inappropriately higher than the objective probability would warrant'.
options.B: Illusion of control is not a recognized bias in the behavioral literature.
options.C: The reading references illusion of control without defining it.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a cognitive error — 'an expectancy of a personal success probability inappropriately higher than the objective probability would warrant'; human tendency to overestimate abilities to control or influence outcomes; could lead to excessive trading and/or heavy weighting on a few stocks; investors should seek contrary viewpoints and set/enforce proper trading and portfolio diversification rules.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The basic philosophy behind active equity management is that investors believe they can control or at least influence outcomes. Skilled investors have a healthy confidence in their own ability to select stocks and influence outcomes, and they expect to outperform the market. The illusion of control bias refers to the human tendency to overestimate these abilities. Langer (1983) defines the illusion of control bias as "an expectancy of a personal success probability inappropriately higher than the objective probability would warrant." The illusion of control is a cognitive error. Having an illusion of control could lead to excessive trading and/or heavy weighting on a few stocks. Investors should seek contrary viewpoints and set and enforce proper trading and portfolio diversification rules to try to avoid this problem.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Langer (1983) named and defined it.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The definition is quoted: expecting personal success with probability inappropriately above the objective odds.

options.A: Overconfidence is the same bias as illusion of control under a different label.
options.B: Overconfidence is not a recognized bias affecting investors.
options.C: emotional bias whereby investors demonstrate unwarranted faith in their own intuitive reasoning, judgment, and/or cognitive abilities — result of overestimating knowledge levels, abilities, and access to information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. They are related but distinct — overconfidence is unwarranted faith in one's reasoning and judgment broadly; illusion of control concerns influencing outcomes.
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is a core EMOTIONAL bias producing underestimated risk and overtrading.
rationales.C:
Full statement: emotional bias whereby investors demonstrate unwarranted faith in their own intuitive reasoning, judgment, and/or cognitive abilities — result of overestimating knowledge levels, abilities, and access to information; unlike illusion of control bias (which is cognitive error), overconfidence bias is illusion of exaggerated knowledge and abilities; investors may attribute success to own ability rather than luck; portfolio manager underestimates risks and overestimates expected returns; regularly reviewing actual investment records and seeking constructive feedback helps.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Overconfidence bias is an emotional bias whereby investors demonstrate unwarranted faith in their own intuitive reasoning, judgment, and/or cognitive abilities. This overconfidence may be the result of overestimating knowledge levels, abilities, and access to information. Unlike the illusion of control bias, which is a cognitive error, overconfidence bias is an illusion of exaggerated knowledge and abilities. Investors may, for example, attribute success to their own ability rather than to luck. Such bias means that the portfolio manager underestimates risks and overestimates expected returns. Regularly reviewing actual investment records and seeking constructive feedback from other professionals can help investors gain awareness of such self-attribution bias.'

options.A: an information-processing bias (cognitive error category) whereby individuals take mental shortcut in estimating probability of outcome based on availability of information and how easily outcome comes to mind.
options.B: Availability bias is an emotional bias driven by attachment to familiar companies.
options.C: Availability bias is not a recognized behavioral phenomenon.

rationales.A:
Full statement: an information-processing bias (cognitive error category) whereby individuals take mental shortcut in estimating probability of outcome based on availability of information and how easily outcome comes to mind; easily recalled outcomes often perceived as being more likely than those harder to recall or understand; in fundamental equity investing, may reduce investment opportunity set and result in insufficient diversification as manager relies on familiar stocks reflecting narrow range of experience; setting appropriate investment strategy in line with investment horizon and conducting disciplined portfolio analysis with long-term focus helps.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Availability bias is an information-processing bias whereby individuals take a mental shortcut in estimating the probability of an outcome based on the availability of the information and how easily the outcome comes to mind. Easily recalled outcomes are often perceived as being more likely than those that are harder to recall or understand. Availability bias falls in the cognitive error category. In fundamental equity investing, this bias may reduce the investment opportunity set and result in insufficient diversification as the portfolio manager relies on familiar stocks that reflect a narrow range of experience. Setting an appropriate investment strategy in line with the investment horizon, as well as conducting a disciplined portfolio analysis with a long-term focus, will help eliminate any short-term over-emphasis caused by this bias.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is an information-processing shortcut — a COGNITIVE error — where ease of recall stands in for probability.
rationales.C: Incorrect. It is standard in the taxonomy: probability judged by how readily examples come to mind.

options.A: (1) company mapping (track many companies over time and across data vendors — each company may have multiple share classes.
options.B: Quantitative investing uses no identifiable categories of data.
options.C: All quantitative data reduce to a single type: historical prices.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) company mapping (track many companies over time and across data vendors — each company may have multiple share classes; new go public, others disappear; identifiers change over time; different vendors have own identifiers); (2) company fundamentals (demographics, financial statements, market data); (3) survey data; (4) unconventional/unstructured data (satellite images, news sentiment, customer-supplier chain metrics, corporate events).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The most commonly used data in quantitative investing typically fall into the following categories: Company mapping is used to track many companies over time and across data vendors. Each company may also have multiple classes of shares. New companies go public, while some existing companies disappear due to bankruptcies, mergers, or takeovers. Company names, ticker symbols, and other identifiers can also change over time. Different data vendors have their own unique identifiers. Company fundamentals include company demographics, financial statements, and other market data (e.g., price, dividends, stock splits, trading volume). Quantitative portfolio managers almost never collect company fundamental data themselves. Instead, they rely on data vendors, such as Capital IQ, Compustat, Worldscope, Reuters, FactSet, and Bloomberg. Survey data include details of corporate earnings, forecasts and estimates by various market participants, macroeconomic variables, sentiment indicators, and information on funds flow. Unconventional data, or unstructured data, include satellite images, measures of news sentiment, customer–supplier chain metrics, and corporate events, among many other types of information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The reading groups the data — company mapping, fundamentals, survey/estimate data, and unconventional/alternative data.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Prices are one input; mapping across vendors and share classes, fundamentals, and alternative data are distinct categories.

options.A: Pearson IC and Spearman rank IC are identical measures computed on the same inputs.
options.B: Pearson IC = simple correlation between factor scores and next period's returns (always between -1 and +1).
options.C: The reading draws no distinction between IC variants.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Pearson correlates factor SCORES with returns and is outlier-sensitive; Spearman correlates RANKS and is robust to outliers.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Pearson IC = simple correlation between factor scores and next period's returns (always between -1 and +1); Spearman rank IC = Pearson correlation between ranked factor scores and ranked forward returns (more robust to outliers); as simple rule of thumb, factor with average monthly IC of 5%-6% considered very strong for US equities; the higher the IC, higher the predictive power.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The Pearson IC is the simple correlation coefficient between the factor scores (essentially standardized exposures) for the current period's and the next period's stock returns. As it is a correlation coefficient, its value is always between –1 and +1 (or, expressed in percentage terms, between –100% and +100%). The higher the IC, the higher the predictive power of the factor for subsequent returns. As a simple rule of thumb, in relation to US equities, any factor with an average monthly IC of 5%–6% is considered very strong. The coefficient is sensitive to outliers, as is illustrated below. A similar but more robust measure is the Spearman rank IC, which is often preferred by practitioners. The Spearman rank IC is essentially the Pearson correlation coefficient between the ranked factor scores and ranked forward returns.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Pearson/Spearman distinction is developed explicitly, including their different sensitivity to extreme values.

options.A: Exhibit 26 shows Pearson and Spearman ICs taking identical values for the earnings-yield signal.
options.B: No IC illustration appears in the reading.
options.C: Pearson IC is negative at -0.8% (single outlier Stock I distorts result), suggesting signal did not perform well and was negatively correlated with subsequent month's returns.

rationales.A: Incorrect. They DIVERGE — a single outlier (Stock I) drags the Pearson IC negative (−0.8%) while the rank IC tells a different story.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The earnings-yield example is the reading's IC illustration, built to show outlier distortion.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Pearson IC is negative at -0.8% (single outlier Stock I distorts result), suggesting signal did not perform well and was negatively correlated with subsequent month's returns; but the sample factor generally in line with subsequent returns except Stock I (highest factor predicted but worst performer); Spearman rank IC at 40% suggests strong predictive power for subsequent returns; long/short tercile portfolio return 0.56% — long basket (Stocks G, H, I) outperformed short basket (Stocks A, B, C) by 56 bps.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the example shown in Exhibit 26 for earnings yield, the Pearson IC is negative at –0.8%, suggesting that the signal did not perform well and was negatively correlated with the subsequent month's returns. Looking more carefully, however, we can see that the sample factor is generally in line with the subsequent stock returns, with the exception of Stock I, for which the factor predicts the highest return but which turns out to be the worst performer. A single outlier can therefore turn what may actually be a good factor into a bad one, as the Pearson IC is sensitive to outliers. In contrast, the Spearman rank IC is at 40%, suggesting that the factor has strong predictive power for subsequent returns. If three equally weighted portfolios had been constructed, the long basket (Stocks G, H, and I) would have outperformed the short basket (Stocks A, B, and C) by 56 bps in this period.'

options.A: Quantitative portfolio construction raises no practical issues once the alpha model is built.
options.B: (1) risk models — estimate variance-covariance matrix of stock returns (directly estimating from sample data typically infeasible with significant estimation errors.
options.C: Risk modeling is unnecessary in quantitative construction because diversification handles all risk.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Risk-model estimation and transaction-cost modeling are named construction issues.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) risk models — estimate variance-covariance matrix of stock returns (directly estimating from sample data typically infeasible with significant estimation errors; managers rely on commercial risk model vendors like MSCI Barra and Axioma); (2) trading costs — two kinds: explicit (commissions, fees, taxes) and implicit (bid-ask spread, market impact); when two stocks have similar expected returns and risks, normally the one with lower execution costs is preferred.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Risk models: Risk models estimate the variance–covariance matrix of stock returns — that is, the risk of every stock and the correlation among stocks. Directly estimating the variance–covariance matrix using sample return data typically is infeasible and suffers from significant estimation errors. Managers generally rely on commercial risk model vendors for these data.' Footnote: 'MSCI Barra and Axioma are examples of data providers.' Also: 'Trading costs: There are two kinds of trading costs — explicit (e.g., commissions, fees, and taxes) and implicit (e.g., bid–ask spread and market impact). When two stocks have similar expected returns and risks, normally the one with lower execution costs is preferred.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Estimating the variance-covariance structure is central — direct sample estimation is typically infeasible at scale, hence factor risk models.

options.A: Survivorship bias, look-ahead bias, data mining, and overfitting pose no risk to quantitative back-tests.
options.B: These four issues affect fundamental analysis but are irrelevant to quantitative processes.
options.C: common pitfalls in quantitative investment processes — survivorship bias (back-tests using only companies currently in business today ignore stocks that left universe due to bankruptcy/delisting/acquisition, leading to overly optimistic results).

rationales.A: Incorrect. They are the canonical quant pitfalls — back-tests on today's survivors, information not yet available, and patterns that fit noise.
rationales.B: Incorrect. They are pitfalls OF quantitative research design specifically.
rationales.C:
Full statement: common pitfalls in quantitative investment processes — survivorship bias (back-tests using only companies currently in business today ignore stocks that left universe due to bankruptcy/delisting/acquisition, leading to overly optimistic results); look-ahead bias (using information unknown or unavailable at time investment decision was made, e.g., accounting data used before released); data mining (excessive search of past financial data to uncover patterns and conform to pre-determined model can introduce bias resulting in model overfitting).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Survivorship bias is one of the most common issues affecting quantitative decision making... When back-tests use only those companies that are currently in business today, they ignore the stocks that have left the investment universe due to bankruptcy, delisting, or acquisition. This approach creates a bias whereby only companies that have survived are tested and it is assumed that the strategy would never have invested in companies that have failed. Survivorship bias often leads to overly optimistic results and sometimes even causes investors to draw wrong conclusions. The second major issue in back-testing is look-ahead bias. This bias results from using information that was unknown or unavailable at the time an investment decision was made. An example of this bias is the use of financial accounting data for a company at a point in time before the data were actually released by the company. In computer science, data mining refers to automated computational processes for discovering patterns in large datasets, often involving sophisticated statistical techniques, computation algorithms, and large-scale database systems. In finance, data mining can refer to such a process and can introduce a bias that results in model overfitting.'

options.A: in theoretical world with no transaction costs, a simple long/short strategy (buying top 20% dividend-paying stocks in Japan with worst performance in previous month and shorting bottom 20% with highest previous-month returns) generated annual return of 12%, beating classic value factor of price to book.
options.B: Transaction costs have no meaningful effect on the profitability of short-term reversal strategies, because the offsetting long and short legs neutralize trading expenses at the portfolio level.
options.C: The reading offers no before-and-after-cost comparison for any factor strategy, discussing implementation costs only in general qualitative terms without a worked market example.

rationales.A:
Full statement: in theoretical world with no transaction costs, a simple long/short strategy (buying top 20% dividend-paying stocks in Japan with worst performance in previous month and shorting bottom 20% with highest previous-month returns) generated annual return of 12%, beating classic value factor of price to book; however if transaction cost assumption changed from 0 bps to 30 bps per trade, return of reversal strategy drops sharply while return of price-to-book strategy drops only modestly.

Correct. Per the reading: 'More importantly, trading is not free. Transaction costs can easily erode returns significantly. An example is the use of short-term reversal as a factor: Stocks that have performed well recently (say, in the last month) are more likely to revert (underperform) in the subsequent month. This reversal factor has been found to be a good stock selection signal in the Japanese equity market (before transaction costs). As shown in Exhibit 27, in a theoretical world with no transaction costs, a simple long/short strategy (buying the top 20% dividend-paying stocks in Japan with the worst performance in the previous month and shorting the bottom 20% stocks with the highest returns in the previous month) has generated an annual return of 12%, beating the classic value factor of price to book. However, if the transaction cost assumption is changed from 0 bps to 30 bps per trade, the return of the reversal strategy drops sharply, while the return of the price-to-book value strategy drops only modestly.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The Japanese reversal example exists to show costs consuming the paper profits of a high-turnover signal.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The example contrasts the theoretical no-cost long/short result with the after-cost reality.

options.A: Equity styles are defined by a single attribute: the price-to-earnings multiple.
options.B: pairs of common attributes — value and growth, large cap and small cap, high volatility and low volatility, high dividend and low dividend, developed markets and emerging markets.
options.C: The reading recognizes no equity styles.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Styles come in attribute PAIRS — value/growth, large/small, high/low volatility, high/low dividend.
rationales.B:
Full statement: pairs of common attributes — value and growth, large cap and small cap, high volatility and low volatility, high dividend and low dividend, developed markets and emerging markets; style pairs need not be mutually exclusive; each pair interprets stock performance from different perspective; combination of several style pairs may give more complete picture of sources of stock returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Equity styles are defined by pairs of common attributes, such as value and growth, large cap and small cap, high volatility and low volatility, high dividend and low dividend, or developed markets and emerging markets. Style pairs need not be mutually exclusive. Each pair interprets the stock performance from a different perspective. A combination of several style pairs may often give a more complete picture of the sources of stock returns.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Style pairs organize much of the reading's analysis.

options.A: Only one approach to style analysis exists — the returns-based method — since portfolio holdings data are proprietary and therefore never available for analytical use.
options.B: The reading identifies no approaches to style analysis, treating style classification as the exclusive province of index providers rather than analysts.
options.C: holdings-based approach (aggregation of attributes from individual stocks in portfolio, more accurate, requires availability of all constituents and style attributes) and returns-based approach (compares returns of strategy to set of style indexes, can be applied when portfolio holdings not disclosed).

rationales.A: Incorrect. TWO approaches are developed: holdings-based and returns-based.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Holdings-based (aggregating constituent attributes, more accurate, data-hungry) and returns-based (regression on style indexes) are both treated.
rationales.C:
Full statement: holdings-based approach (aggregation of attributes from individual stocks in portfolio, more accurate, requires availability of all constituents and style attributes) and returns-based approach (compares returns of strategy to set of style indexes, can be applied when portfolio holdings not disclosed); Morningstar and Thomson Reuters Lipper use 0/1 style attribute; MSCI and FTSE Russell allow stocks to have characteristics of two styles at once via multifactor approach with style inclusion factors.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Two main approaches are often used in style analysis: a holdings-based approach and a returns-based approach. Each approach has its own strengths and weaknesses.' And: 'An equity investment style is actually the aggregation of attributes from individual stocks in the portfolio. Holdings-based approaches to style analysis are done bottom-up, but they are executed differently by the various commercial investment information providers. Using different criteria or different sources of underlying value and growth numbers may lead to slightly different classifications for stocks... In the style classification process followed by Morningstar and Thomson Reuters Lipper, the styles of individual stocks are clearly defined in that a stock's attribute for a specific style is 1 if it is included in that style index; otherwise, it is 0. The methodology used by MSCI and FTSE Russell, on the other hand, assumes that a stock can have characteristics of two styles, such as value and growth, at the same time. This methodology uses a multifactor approach to assign style inclusion factors to each stock.'

options.A: Morningstar's value score derives from a single factor: trailing price-to-earnings.
options.B: Morningstar publishes no documented style-scoring methodology.
options.C: value score = 50% forward-looking (price to projected earnings, 50%) + 50% historical (price to book 12.5%, price to sales 12.5%, price to cash flow 12.5%, dividend yield 12.5%).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The score blends forward-looking (price to projected earnings, 50%) and historical components (P/B, P/S, P/CF, dividend yield).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exhibit 31 documents the component weights explicitly.
rationales.C:
Full statement: value score = 50% forward-looking (price to projected earnings, 50%) + 50% historical (price to book 12.5%, price to sales 12.5%, price to cash flow 12.5%, dividend yield 12.5%); growth score = 50% forward-looking (long-term projected earnings growth, 50%) + 50% historical (historical earnings growth 12.5%, sales growth 12.5%, cash flow growth 12.5%, book value growth 12.5%); scores scaled 0-100, difference = net style score; strongly negative → value, strongly positive → growth, close to zero → core.

Correct. Per Exhibit 31 (Morningstar Value and Growth Scoring Scheme): Value Score Components: 'Forward-looking measures 50.0%: Price to projected earnings 50.0%. Historical measures 50.0%: Price to book 12.5%, Price to sales 12.5%, Price to cash flow 12.5%, Dividend yield 12.5%.' Growth Score Components: 'Forward-looking measures 50.0%: Long-term projected earnings growth 50.0%. Historical measures 50.0%: Historical earnings growth 12.5%, Sales growth 12.5%, Cash flow growth 12.5%, Book value growth 12.5%.' Per the reading: 'The scores are scaled to a range of 0 to 100, and the difference between the stock's growth and value scores is called the net style score. If this net style score is strongly negative, approaching –100, the stock's style is classified as value. If the result is strongly positive, the stock is classified as growth. If the scores for value and growth are similar in strength, the net style score will be close to zero and the stock will be classified as core.'

options.A: value defined using three variables: book-value-to-price ratio, 12-month forward-earnings-to-price ratio, dividend yield.
options.B: MSCI defines its value and growth indexes without reference to any specified variables.
options.C: MSCI's value definition rests on one variable: dividend yield alone.

rationales.A:
Full statement: value defined using three variables: book-value-to-price ratio, 12-month forward-earnings-to-price ratio, dividend yield; growth defined using five variables: long-term forward EPS growth rate, short-term forward EPS growth rate, current internal growth rate, long-term historical EPS growth trend, long-term historical sales-per-share growth trend; z-scores calculated and aggregated for each security to determine overall style characteristics; a 'value inclusion factor' of 0.6 means stock contributes 60% to value index and 40% to growth index.

Correct. Per the reading's MSCI section: 'The value investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using three variables: book-value-to-price ratio, 12-month forward-earnings-to-price ratio, and dividend yield. The growth investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using five variables: long-term forward EPS growth rate, short-term forward EPS growth rate, current internal growth rate, long-term historical EPS growth trend, and long-term historical sales-per-share growth trend. Z-scores for each variable are calculated and aggregated for each security to determine the security's overall style characteristics. For example, a stock is assigned a so-called "value inclusion factor" of 0.6, which means that the stock could have both value and growth characteristics and contributes to the performance of the value and growth indexes by 60% and 40%, respectively.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The variables are specified — three for value, five for growth.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Value uses book-to-price, forward earnings-to-price, AND dividend yield together.

options.A: the difference in weights between the active portfolio and the benchmark, expressed as R_A = Σ ΔW_i × R_i where ΔW_i is the active weight (portfolio weight minus benchmark weight) and R_i is the return on security i.
options.B: Active return is driven entirely by the benchmark's own returns, independent of portfolio weights.
options.C: Active return bears no relation to the differences between portfolio and benchmark weights.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The excess return — also called active return (R_A) — of an actively managed portfolio is driven by the difference in weights between the active portfolio and the benchmark. It can be mathematically expressed as R_A = Σ ΔW_i × R_i where R_i = the return on security i and ΔW_i = the difference between the portfolio weights W_Pi and the benchmark weights W_Bi. ΔW_i is also referred to as the active weight.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Benchmark return is the reference point; ACTIVE return comes from active weights times security returns.
rationales.C: Incorrect. R_A = Σ ΔW_i × R_i — active weights are precisely what generate it.

options.A: Actively managed portfolios always generate positive active returns by construction.
options.B: gains from overweighting outperforming securities and underweighting underperforming securities are greater than losses from underweighting outperforming securities and overweighting underperforming securities.
options.C: No principle governs when active returns turn positive.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Active management is zero-sum around the benchmark; positivity requires the condition, not the label.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'An active manager will generate positive active returns if: The gains generated by overweighting the securities that outperform the benchmark and underweighting the securities that underperform the benchmark are, on average, > The losses generated by underweighting the securities that outperform the benchmark and overweighting the securities that underperform the benchmark.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The principle is definite: gains from correct over/underweights must exceed losses from incorrect ones.

options.A: investment risks for which investors expect to be compensated through a long-run return premium (such as exposure to market risk and liquidity risk).
options.B: Rewarded factors encompass any risk a portfolio happens to carry, compensated or not.
options.C: Rewarded factors are risks that systematically REDUCE long-run returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: investment risks for which investors expect to be compensated through a long-run return premium (such as exposure to market risk and liquidity risk); existence well documented and supported by strong empirical evidence; recognition fundamentally altering investment management industry with large asset owners negotiating fee structures compensating managers for returns above/beyond simple rewarded factor exposure.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In this reading, we use "rewarded factors" as a generic term that refers specifically to investment risks for which investors expect to be compensated through a long-run return premium, such as exposure to market risk and liquidity risk. The existence of numerous rewarded factors is well documented in the literature and supported by strong empirical evidence. The recognition of this phenomenon is fundamentally altering the investment management industry, with large asset owners negotiating fee structures that compensate active managers for returns above and beyond those that can be generated by simple exposure to rewarded factors.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The defining property is expected COMPENSATION — a long-run return premium; uncompensated risks are the contrast class.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rewarded means premium-earning, such as market and liquidity exposure — not return-reducing.

options.A: The fundamental law of active management has no formal mathematical expression and is instead a qualitative principle stating that skilled managers should diversify their bets across as many securities as practical.
options.B: The fundamental law states that expected active return equals the information coefficient alone, so a manager's forecasting accuracy fully determines active performance without regard to breadth or implementation.
options.C: E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC, where IC = expected information coefficient (extent to which forecasted active returns correspond to realized), BR = breadth (number of truly independent decisions made each year), TC = transfer coefficient (ability to translate insights into decisions without constraint.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The law is an explicit equation: E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC.
rationales.B: Incorrect. IC is one input; breadth, active risk, and the transfer coefficient complete the expression.
rationales.C:
Full statement: E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC, where IC = expected information coefficient (extent to which forecasted active returns correspond to realized), BR = breadth (number of truly independent decisions made each year), TC = transfer coefficient (ability to translate insights into decisions without constraint; unconstrained portfolio TC = 1), σ_RA = manager's active risk.

Correct. Per the reading Equation 4: 'E(R_A) = IC × √BR × σ_RA × TC where IC = Expected information coefficient of the manager — the extent to which a manager's forecasted active returns correspond to the managers realized active returns; BR = Breadth — the number of truly independent decisions made each year; TC = Transfer coefficient, or the ability to translate portfolio insights into investment decisions without constraint (a truly unconstrained portfolio would have a transfer coefficient of 1); σ_RA = the manager's active risk.' Footnote: 'The basic fundamental law was initially introduced by Grinold (1989) but was further expanded into the full fundamental law with the addition of the transfer coefficient by Clarke, de Silva, and Thorley (2002).'

options.A: manager could expect to generate active return of 15 bps yearly on average with single independent decision (0.10 × √1 × 6% × 0.25 = 0.15%).
options.B: The worked example is trivial, showing only that zero decisions produce zero return.
options.C: The example provides no numerical answer.

rationales.A:
Full statement: manager could expect to generate active return of 15 bps yearly on average with single independent decision (0.10 × √1 × 6% × 0.25 = 0.15%); to achieve 1% excess return would need approximately 40 fully independent decisions (0.10 × √40 × 6% × 0.25 ≈ 0.95% ≈ 1%); constraint TC 0.25 representative of a constrained long-only investor.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, assuming an active risk of 6% (which many institutional investors would consider to be high), a transfer coefficient of 0.25 (representative of a constrained long-only investor), and an information coefficient of 0.10, the manager could expect to generate an active return of 15 bps yearly, on average, if she makes a single independent decision. If the manager wanted to achieve excess return of 1%, she would need to make approximately 40 fully independent decisions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. It quantifies a single decision's value: 0.10 × √1 × 6% × 0.25 = 15 bps of expected annual active return.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The answer is explicit — 15 bps per single independent decision at IC 0.10, TC 0.25, active risk 6%.

options.A: Diversification confers unlimited benefits, driving portfolio risk to zero as holdings increase.
options.B: Position sizing faces no diversification-related limits.
options.C: diversification is powerful tool but has limitations — even most diversified portfolio of high-volatility stocks (500-stock portfolio with 30% avg single-stock vol) cannot achieve same level of vol as portfolios of lower-vol stocks.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Systematic risk survives any number of holdings — a broad portfolio of high-volatility stocks remains volatile.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The limit is the point: idiosyncratic risk diversifies away, but common-factor volatility caps what sizing dispersion can achieve.
rationales.C:
Full statement: diversification is powerful tool but has limitations — even most diversified portfolio of high-volatility stocks (500-stock portfolio with 30% avg single-stock vol) cannot achieve same level of vol as portfolios of lower-vol stocks; even most concentrated portfolio of lower-vol stocks displays portfolio vol lower than highly diversified portfolio of higher-vol stocks; concentrated portfolio bears higher idiosyncratic risk.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Examining this table closely, we can see that diversification is a powerful tool but that it has its limitations. Even the most diversified portfolio of high-volatility stocks (the 500-stock portfolio with an average single-stock volatility of 30%) cannot achieve the same level of volatility inherent in the portfolios of lower-volatility stocks. Even the most concentrated portfolio of lower-volatility stocks displays a portfolio volatility lower than that of the highly diversified portfolio of higher-volatility stocks. The concentrated portfolio, however, bears higher idiosyncratic risk, which can substantially influence portfolio performance.'

options.A: Systematic strategies rely on purely discretionary judgment applied position by position.
options.B: designed around construction of portfolios seeking to extract return premiums from balanced exposure to known rewarded factors.
options.C: Systematic strategies operate without rules of any kind.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Discretionary judgment is the CONTRASTING approach; systematic strategies run on rules.
rationales.B:
Full statement: designed around construction of portfolios seeking to extract return premiums from balanced exposure to known rewarded factors; typically incorporate research-based rules across broad universe of securities; seek to reduce exposure to idiosyncratic risk and often use broadly diversified portfolios; typically more adaptable to a formal portfolio optimization process; unlikely to run concentrated portfolios.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Systematic strategies are more likely to be designed around the construction of portfolios seeking to extract return premiums from a balanced exposure to known, rewarded factors... Systematic strategies typically incorporate research-based rules across a broad universe of securities... Systematic strategies seek to reduce exposure to idiosyncratic risk and often use broadly diversified portfolios to achieve the desired factor exposure while minimizing security-specific risk... Systematic strategies are typically more adaptable to a formal portfolio optimization process.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rules are their essence — balanced, repeatable exposure to known rewarded factors.

options.A: Systematic and discretionary managers operate under entirely unrelated frameworks with nothing in common.
options.B: each investment approach is implemented within a framework that specifies acceptable levels of active risk and Active Share relative to a clearly articulated benchmark (Active Share is a measure of how similar a portfolio is to its benchmark).
options.C: Neither systematic nor discretionary managers operate within any framework.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Both are implemented within a framework specifying acceptable active risk and Active Share versus a benchmark.
rationales.B:
Full statement: each investment approach is implemented within a framework that specifies acceptable levels of active risk and Active Share relative to a clearly articulated benchmark (Active Share is a measure of how similar a portfolio is to its benchmark); manager may emphasize these dimensions to varying degrees to differentiate portfolio from benchmark; approaches also vary in extent to which they are benchmark aware versus benchmark agnostic.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In addition, these approaches can vary in the extent to which they are benchmark aware versus benchmark agnostic. Each manager's investment approach is implemented within a framework that specifies the acceptable levels of active risk and Active Share relative to a clearly articulated benchmark. (Active Share is a measure of how similar a portfolio is to its benchmark.) A manager may emphasize these dimensions to varying degrees as he attempts to differentiate his portfolio from the benchmark.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. A common risk/Active Share framework disciplines both approaches.

options.A: Discretionary strategies are purely systematic, applying fixed rules without judgment.
options.B: Discretionary and systematic strategies are identical in process and philosophy.
options.C: search for active returns by building greater depth of understanding of firm's governance, business model, and competitive landscape, through better factor proxies (e.g., better definition of Quality), or through successful timing strategies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Depth of judgment about governance, business models, and competition is the discretionary hallmark.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Depth-through-judgment versus breadth-through-rules is the core contrast.
rationales.C:
Full statement: search for active returns by building greater depth of understanding of firm's governance, business model, and competitive landscape, through better factor proxies (e.g., better definition of Quality), or through successful timing strategies; integrate judgment of manager on smaller subset of securities; generally more concentrated portfolios reflecting depth of manager's insights; less formal approach to portfolio construction (building portfolio of securities deemed attractive subject to agreed-upon risk constraints).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Discretionary strategies search for active returns by building a greater depth of understanding of a firm's governance, business model, and competitive landscape, through the development of better factor proxies (e.g., a better definition of Quality), or through successful timing strategies. Factor timing is a challenging endeavor, and few factor-based systematic strategies have integrated a factor timing approach... Discretionary strategies integrate the judgment of the manager, usually on a smaller subset of securities... Discretionary strategies are generally more concentrated portfolios, reflecting the depth of the manager's insights on company characteristics and the competitive landscape... Discretionary portfolio managers typically use a less formal approach to portfolio construction, building a portfolio of securities deemed attractive, subject to a set of agreed-upon risk constraints.'

options.A: top-down seeks to understand overall geo-political/economic/financial/social/public policy environment then project effect on countries/asset classes/sectors/securities.
options.B: Top-down and bottom-up approaches are indistinguishable in practice.
options.C: Neither top-down nor bottom-up describes an actual investment strategy.

rationales.A:
Full statement: top-down seeks to understand overall geo-political/economic/financial/social/public policy environment then project effect on countries/asset classes/sectors/securities; bottom-up develops understanding of environment by first evaluating risk-return characteristics of individual securities; both rely on returns from factors but top-down emphasizes macro factors while bottom-up emphasizes security-specific factors.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A top-down approach seeks to understand the overall geo-political, economic, financial, social, and public policy environment and then project how the expected environment will affect countries, asset classes, sectors, and then securities. An investment manager who projects that growth companies will outperform value companies, that financials will outperform industrials, that the US market will outperform the European market, that oil prices will increase, or that cash will outperform equity and then targets individual securities and/or a cash/stock allocation to reflect these views is following a top-down approach. A manager following a bottom-up approach develops his understanding of the environment by first evaluating the risk and return characteristics of individual securities... Both top-down and bottom-up strategies typically rely on returns from factors. However, top-down managers are more likely to emphasize macro factors, whereas bottom-up managers emphasize security-specific factors.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The starting point differs fundamentally — macro environment projected downward versus security-level analysis aggregated upward.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both are standard, well-defined strategy architectures.

options.A: Portfolio construction proceeds without any objective function.
options.B: The reading specifies no framework for construction objectives.
options.C: maximize risk-adjusted return — if risk measured by predicted active risk, objective seeks to maximize information ratio (ratio of active return to active risk).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Maximizing risk-adjusted return is the common objective.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The framework is explicit — with predicted active risk as the risk measure, the objective is maximizing the information ratio.
rationales.C:
Full statement: maximize risk-adjusted return — if risk measured by predicted active risk, objective seeks to maximize information ratio (ratio of active return to active risk); if risk measured by predicted portfolio volatility, objective seeks to maximize Sharpe ratio (ratio of return in excess of risk-free rate to portfolio volatility); ideally would specify net returns adjusted for implementation costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A common objective function in portfolio management is to maximize a risk-adjusted return. If risk is being measured by predicted active risk, then the objective function is seeking to maximize the information ratio (the ratio of active return to active risk). If risk is being measured by predicted portfolio volatility, then the objective function is seeking to maximize the Sharpe ratio (the ratio of return in excess of the risk-free rate to portfolio volatility). Ideally, these objective functions would specify net returns — adjusted for the costs associated with implementation.'

options.A: Systematic and discretionary managers specify objectives and constraints with identical explicitness.
options.B: The reading draws no distinction in how the two manager types state objectives.
options.C: objectives and constraints of systematic managers are explicitly specified whereas those of discretionary managers are less explicitly specified.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Systematic specifications are EXPLICIT (coded into the process); discretionary ones are less formally specified.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The explicit-versus-implicit specification difference is drawn directly.
rationales.C:
